scope: Wire the App menu and keyboard commands: ⌘R refresh, ⌘⇧F fetch, ⌘⇧P pull, ⌘⇧K push, ⌘⇧N new branch, ⌘N new window; ⌘, is auto-wired by Settings scene; ⌘T deferred (no branch picker UI).

architectureReview.summary: Clean separation. AppCommands is a thin @MainActor @Observable service bridging menu actions to existing NetworkOps / BranchOps / RepoStore / RepoSidebarModel. It holds no new state except a newBranchRequest UUID used as a sheet-open signal. KiteCommands reads it via @FocusedValue(\.appCommands); KiteApp injects via .focusedSceneValue on the WindowGroup and plain .environment on Settings. Window id "main" lets ⌘N spawn a second identical instance via openWindow(id:).
goodPractices: Established @Observable + @MainActor + @ObservationIgnored discipline on injected dependencies — no retain-cycle risk.
FocusedValue mechanism correctly routes a second ⌘N window's shortcuts to THAT window's service (same AppCommands instance since it's app-level state).
openNewBranchSheet() uses a UUID bump — every menu invocation produces a new value so .onChange fires reliably even if the sheet was dismissed in between.

pushFocused() mirrors PushToolbarButton semantics exactly: .needsUpstream produces a toast redirecting the user to the toolbar (which owns the set-upstream confirmation sheet), avoiding a second sheet entry point.
AppCommandsTests covers 7 cases with REAL fixture repos and exercises observable side-effects (origin/main advancing, bare's main ref, lastChangeAt bumping, newBranchRequest UUID regeneration).

shortcutCoverage.⌘⇧N: wired — appCommands.openNewBranchSheet() bumps newBranchRequest; NewBranchButton observes and opens sheet
shortcutCoverage.⌘N: wired — CommandGroup(replacing: .newItem) Button with keyboardShortcut("n", modifiers: .command) → openWindow(id: "main")
shortcutCoverage.⌘,: SwiftUI auto-wires via the Settings {} scene — not in KiteCommands
shortcutCoverage.⌘T: DEFERRED — no branch picker UI in v1; documented in KiteCommands doc comment and features.json

issues.description: @FocusedValue(\.appCommands) captures the frontmost window's AppCommands value via .focusedSceneValue. Only the primary WindowGroup has that injection — the Settings scene does NOT call .focusedSceneValue(\.appCommands, ...). If the Settings window is the frontmost window when a shortcut fires, @FocusedValue resolves to nil and menu items show disabled. This is probably intentional (you can't push from Settings), but it is observable behavior: with Settings frontmost, every Repository-menu item is disabled. Scrutiny: acceptable. Worth a one-line comment clarifying intent.

CommandGroup(replacing: .newItem) puts a single "New Window" button in File menu. SwiftUI's default .newItem group also contains "Close" and "Save" placeholder items — replacing wipes all of them. File menu is now {New Window}. Acceptable for v1 (no documents), but the author may want `CommandGroup(after: .newItem)` + a `CommandGroup(replacing: .newItem) { EmptyView() }` combo to preserve standard items if macOS 16 introduces useful ones.

pushFocused() captures `store.focus` at entry and then awaits readCurrentBranch. If focus swaps mid-await, the push runs against the originally-focused repo. Per the handoff's discoveredIssue #3 this is intentional (matches toolbar button behavior) but it should be written as an explicit local binding `let focus = store.focus` — the current code refers to `focus` in scope but reads `store.focus` twice, so two consecutive reads can produce different values. Low risk given the synchronous MainActor guarantee but tighten by hoisting.

forceRefresh() lives in Sources/Repo/ which per INTERFACES.md §3 is git-engine-worker territory, but was authored by a swiftui-worker feature. The method is UI-driven (⌘R trigger) with no git semantics. Functionally fine; queue INTERFACES.md §3 amendment to recognize this crossover or re-home to a Sources/ViewModels wrapper.

Button closures capture `let commands = appCommands` before the Task, which is fine, but the same pattern is repeated 5 times with subtle variations. Could be factored into a private helper `dispatch(_ action: @escaping (AppCommands) async -> Void)` returning a Button closure. Not blocking — repetition is explicit and readable.

⌘N "New Window" is enabled unconditionally, including when the built-in ⌘N (via .newItem) might not have done so in a document-based app. Given Kite isn't document-based, this is correct — a user always can spawn another window regardless of focus. Worth noting, no change needed.

The .onChange(of: appCommands.newBranchRequest) compares UUID values; on a nil → UUID transition it opens. Subsequent UUID → UUID transitions also open. UUID → nil would not open (guard). This is correct since openNewBranchSheet never writes nil, but a defensive comment locking the contract would help future maintainers.

testsEvidence.unitTestsRun: AppCommands suite: 7/7 green (refreshFocused bumps lastChangeAt; fetch/pull/push drive NetworkOps with REAL fixture repos; openNewBranchSheet bumps UUID; hasFocus tracks store.focus; async actions are no-ops without focus).
testsEvidence.xcuiAuthoredButSkipped: CommandsUITests.swift — 7 XCTSkipIf-guarded test cases: refresh/fetch/pull/push/new-branch shortcuts, ⌘N new window, ⌘, settings. Consistent with the project-wide TCC deferment pattern (FetchUITests, CreateBranchUITests, etc.).

validationAssertionsFulfilled.VAL-UI-002: PASSED (menu parity with toolbar for refresh/fetch/pull/push/new-branch; verified via AppCommands unit tests routing to NetworkOps + BranchOps)
validationAssertionsFulfilled.VAL-UI-003: PARTIAL (6 of 7 documented shortcuts wired; ⌘T deferred per features.json). Unit tests cover route-through. XCUITest authored but TCC-skipped.
validationAssertionsFulfilled.VAL-UI-009: PASSED architecturally (WindowGroup id="main" + openWindow(id:) in a Button with ⌘N keyboardShortcut). XCUITest authored but TCC-skipped.

deferredItems.⌘T-switch-branch: No branch picker UI in v1. Documented as deferment in features.json + KiteCommands doc comment. Queue as v2 candidate.
